definition: An element q = a + bi + cj + dk of H with three imaginary basis directions.
A quaternion of norm 1; unit quaternions form S^3.
The unit-quaternion 3-sphere used as the compact QSG prototype.
The compact matrix group isomorphic to unit quaternions and used for complex-linear representation.
The QSG model manifold with normalized quaternionic orientation and a real logarithmic scale coordinate.
The real coordinate s = log(rho/rho_*) that converts multiplicative dilation into additive translation.
The unit-quaternion form q = cos(phi) + n-hat sin(phi), where n-hat is the imaginary rotor axis and is distinct from s.
The algebra A of functions or observables represented on a state space.
The complex spinor, matrix-valued, finite, or truncated state space H used in the formal model.
An operator D whose spectrum and commutators encode the chosen hypersphere and scale geometry.
The expression [D, pi(a)] measuring variation of an observable under D.
A candidate object (A, H, D) with spectral-triple checks such as bounded commutators and compact resolvent or a finite analogue.
A profile, potential, region, or finite ladder along the scale coordinate that selects or localizes modes into preferred scale bands.
A localized eigenmode chi_n(s) along the scale coordinate, distinct from an S^3 harmonic.
Anchor-Generating Quaternionic Factorial; a claim-scoped extension lens for scale-localized resonance wells.
Persistence of phase, orientation, scale localization, resonance level, or spectral relation under a stated action.
